segments.s545-g33: ازان پس is a two-word temporal/discourse marker meaning 'thereupon / after that' (ازان = 'thereupon'; پس = 'after, then'). خروشی برآورد سخت is a short clause in which خروشی (a noun 'a cry/roar') is the subject and برآورد (past/implied verbal form 'raised, uttered' in classical/poetic usage) is the verb; سخت is an adverbial modifier 'strongly/harshly' modifying how the cry was raised. Word order follows classical Persian: temporal phrase → subject → verb → adverbial.
segments.s545-g34: کزو introduces the following result clause and means 'from it / from which' (referring back to the cry). خیره شد is a verb phrase 'became astonished / was stunned' (شد = 'became'). شاه پیروز بخت is the subject noun phrase following the verb in inverted/poetic order: 'the king, fortunate and victorious.' This inversion (verb before subject) is common in poetry.
segments.s545-g35: به موبد چنین گفت is 'he said thus to the mōbed' (به + object, موبد = 'mōbed/priest', چنین = 'thus/so'). Here گفت is the reporting verb introducing direct speech; in the supplied senses none corresponds to 'said', so the sense index is marked -1.
segments.s545-g36: نگه کن is an imperative 'look / keep watch' (نگه = gaze/watch; کن = imperative 'do'). که تا ... نژاد begins a subordinate clause introduced by که + تا meaning 'so that / in order that' (here both introduce the content: 'see/so that you know from whom ...'). از که دارد نژاد = 'from whom he has lineage' with از = 'from', که = relative 'who/whom', دارد = present 'has', نژاد = 'lineage'.
segments.s545-g37: بپرسید موبد is 'the mōbed asked' (بپرسید = past-tense verb 'asked'; موبد = subject). ندانست کس is a compact clause meaning 'no one knew' with ندانست = 'did not know' (past of دانستن) and کس here functioning as 'anyone / no one' in the negative context (i.e., 'no one'). Word order is verb → subject/indefinite pronoun because of poetic compression.
segments.s545-g38: همه خامشی برگزیدند و بس: همه = 'everyone/all', خامشی = 'silence' (object), برگزیدند = 'they chose/selected' (past plural). و بس at the end is a closing formula 'and that was enough / and that is all' (بس = 'enough'). Together: 'All chose silence, and that was enough.'
segments.s545-g39: به موبد چنین گفت پس شهریار = 'Then the king said thus to the mōbed' (به + mobed = 'to the mōbed'; چنین گفت = 'said thus'; پس شهریار = 'then/the king' with پس marking sequence). As above, گفت is the reporting verb 'said' but the provided sense entries don't include 'said', so its sense index is -1.
segments.s545-g40: که introduces the content clause: بردارش از خاک و نزد من آر is an imperative sentence. بردارش is بردار (imperative 'take/bring') + -ش (third-person possessive 'his') = 'bring his brother' (archaic: بردار also 'brother' in other contexts, but here verbal). از خاک = 'from the earth/from the ground' (از = 'from', خاک = 'earth'). نزد من آر is an imperative 'bring to my presence / bring before me' (نزد = 'in the presence of', من = 'me'). The final آر is an imperative form 'bring/bring to' in archaic/poetic usage; the supplied senses do not include this 'bring' meaning, so its sense is -1.
segments.s545-g9: چنین (thus) is an adverb introducing the result; تا + برآمد gives a temporal subordinate meaning 'until ... arose/passed'. برآمد here is a past verb 'arose/came to pass' governing the following noun phrase. برین is the demonstrative compound = بر + این, 'those/aforementioned' (بر as an older locative/emphatic element and این as the demonstrative), and سال is the noun 'years'. Word order is literary: temporal clause 'چنین تا برآمد برین هفت سال' = 'thus until those seven years had passed'.
segments.s545-g10: ببود is an archaic past copula meaning 'was'. اورمزد is the subject 'Ahura Mazda'. از here is the preposition 'from/of' used idiomatically with جهان 'world' to indicate location/realm, and بی‌همال is an adjective 'without pause/unceasing' describing the manner. Together they form the main clause 'ببود اورمزد از جهان بی‌همال' = 'Ahura Mazda was in the world without respite.'
segments.s545-g11: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking the agent/source ('from everyone'). هرکس here means 'everybody' (each person). نهانش is 'his being hidden' or idiomatically 'him hidden' (نهان + possessive -ش). همی is the old progressive/continuous marker 'still/continually' and داشتند is the past plural 'they had/kept' (used here as 'they kept [him]'). Together: 'ز هرکس نهانش همی داشتند' = 'they kept him hidden from everyone.'
segments.s545-g12: به جایی is 'to/at any place' (به + جایی), ببازیش is a noun/adverbial 'for amusement/for play' (بِ-بازیش), and نگذاشتند is the past negative 'they did not allow/they did not leave'. The clause as a whole means 'and they did not let him be placed anywhere for play' = 'they did not leave him anywhere for amusement.'
segments.s545-g13: به نخچیر شد is a verbal phrase 'became prey/was taken as game' (به + نخچیر + شد). The temporal phrase هفت روز 'seven days' indicates duration and follows the verb in this poetic order, while اردشیر is the patient/subject (name placed after the time expression). Literal order: 'To game became, for seven days, Ardashir' = 'Ardashir became the quarry for seven days.'
segments.s545-g14: بشد is a past/inchoative form 'became' (here 'it came about that / he became'); نیز means 'also/likewise'. شاپور is the name and نخچیرگیر is a compound noun 'nakhchīr-gīr' meaning 'hunter' (literally 'one who takes the quarry'). Together: 'بشد نیز شاپور نخچیرگیر' = 'and Shapur too became the hunter.'
segments.s545-g15: نهان here is an adverb meaning 'secretly' (rather than adjectival 'hidden'), placed before the name for emphasis. اورمزد is the subject 'Ahura Mazda'. از میان means 'out of/from among' and گروه is 'the group/company/troop' — so the phrase is 'نهان اورمزد از میان گروه' = 'secretly Ahura Mazda out of the midst of the group.'
segments.s545-g16: بیامد is the past 'came forth'. کز is the literary relative 'that/from which' introducing the reason clause. آموختن here functions in the sense 'to teach' (classical use), شد is 'became', and ستوه is 'weary/tired'. The relative clause 'کز آموختن شد ستوه' therefore means 'for he became weary of teaching.'
segments.s545-g57: پسر is the subject 'the son'. باید is the modal 'ought/should' and takes the following subjunctive/optative clause. از هرک is a prepositional phrase 'from/of any (one/stock)', placed before the verb in classical word order. باشد is the subjunctive of 'to be' agreeing with the modal باید, and رواست (روا + است contracted) is a following comment/predicate meaning 'it is fitting/permissible' — a separate evaluative assertion attached to the previous clause.
segments.s545-g58: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that/for that they say'. گویند is the impersonal/historical 'they say' (reporting verb). کاین is the older fused form 'که این' (here functioning as 'that this' / 'that which' or simply 'this'), followed by بچه 'child'. پادشاست is an archaic orthography of 'پادشاه است' meaning 'is a king' (copula + predicate).
segments.s545-g59: بدو is the clitic/prepositional 'to him' (indirect object) placed before the verb for emphasis. گفت is the speech verb 'said' (classical epic uses inverted order: indirect object before verb). شاپور is the speaker's name. نوشه is the object 'a draught/nectar' and بدی here is an archaic 2nd-person past form 'you gave' (transitive), so the clause reads roughly 'Shapur said to him: "(you) gave a draught".'
segments.s545-g60: جهان را is the direct object 'the world' marked by the object marker را. به دیدار is a prepositional phrase 'for the meeting/visit' (به + noun). توشه is 'provisions' or 'store' and functions as the thing given 'provision for the meeting'. بدی again is the archaic past 'you gave' understood to govern both نوشه and توشه; the verb may be omitted or repeated stylistically.
segments.s545-g61: ز پشت (poetic 'ز' = از) + پشت 'back/behind' is a prepositional phrase 'from the back/of my back' placed before the copula. منست here functions as a fused predicate (classical/enclitic style) meaning 'is of me / belongs to me' (literally 'is mine' or 'comes from me'). و نام اورمزد links by 'and' to state the name: 'and the name [is] Ohrmazd' (اورمزد = Ahura Mazda).
segments.s545-g62: درخشنده is an adjectival/predicative participle 'shining/radiant'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as'. لاله is the simile's vehicle 'a tulip' (a conventional Persian image of beauty). اندر + فرزد ('in/among youths/sons') completes the simile: 'radiant like a tulip among youths.' Word order is typical epic: adjective + simile + location.
segments.s545-g63: نهان is an adverb/adjective 'hidden/secretly' placed before the verb for emphasis. داشتم is the past-tense form 'I had / I kept' (here auxiliary-like 'I kept [it]'). چندش is the object 'disgust/repugnance' (feeling), and از شهریار indicates the target/source 'toward the king / from the king' — together: 'I kept my disgust hidden from the king.'
segments.s545-g64: بدان is here the classical compound of the prefix بـ + دان (functioning adverbially/relationally) meaning roughly 'to that/for that' or 'about that'. تا introduces the temporal clause 'until'. برآید is a present-subjunctive/impersonal 'it arise/come forth / be revealed'. بر از combines the preposition بر 'upon/about' with از 'from' to indicate relation, and میوه‌دار 'fruit-bearer/fruitful one' is the agent/source referenced. Together: '...until it was revealed concerning/from the fruit-bearer.'
segments.s545-g97: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. شاپور is the proper name (subject). رفت is the past tense verb 'went/withdrew'. اندر + آرام خویش is a prepositional phrase: اندر 'into/in' + آرام 'rest/peace' with the enclitic possessive ش (written here split as خوی + ش) giving آرام خویش 'his own repose' or 'his rest'. Word order is typical older Persian: temporal clause (چو ...) + subject + verb + locative phrase.
segments.s545-g98: ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from/of' and combines with گیتی 'the world' to form 'of the world'; ندیده is a past participle meaning 'not seen' or 'having seen nothing' and functions as the verb phrase 'he had seen nothing'. به جز is an idiom 'except', where به is part of the construction and جز is the core 'except'. کام خویش = کام + خوی + ش (desire + self + his) = 'his own desire'. The whole phrase means 'having seen nothing of the world except his own desire.'
segments.s545-g99: زمین هفت کشور is a noun phrase 'the earth, the seven countries' (classical Persian often orders noun + numeral + counted noun). مرا is the dative/benefactive expression 'for me' (here 'became mine'); گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned', and راست is an adverbial intensifier 'true/indeed'. Thus literally 'the earth's seven countries became (turned) to me, indeed.'
segments.s545-g100: دلم is دل + enclitic 'م' = 'my heart' (possessive). یافت is past 'found/obtained' (here 'obtained'). از بخت is a prepositional phrase 'from fortune/fate' (از = 'from', بخت = 'fate/fortune'). چیزی 'something' is the indefinite direct object; که introduces a relative clause 'that', and خواست is past 'wanted/desired'. Word order: possessor+verb+source+object+relative clause.
segments.s545-g101: وزان پس is a temporal connector 'and from that / and then' (وزان = 'and from that', پس = 'then/afterward'). بر governs the following noun phrase and here marks relation 'over/upon' or 'concerning' کارداران اوی 'his administrators/officials' (کارداران = managers/officials, اوی is an enclitic referring back to 'him/his'). The phrase sets up the people or sphere affected before the verbal action.
segments.s545-g102: شهنشاه is the title 'Shahanshah' (king of kings/emperor). کردند is the past plural 'they made/did' (here 'they conferred' or 'they proclaimed'). عنوان اوی means 'the title of him' or 'his title' (عنوان = 'title', اوی = 'his/for him'). The sequence places the title and verb before the possessive clitic, so the sense is 'they made/gave him the title Shahanshah.'
segments.s545-g65: گرانمایه (here a substantive/adjective used as a noun, "the precious one") stands first for emphasis; از + NP expresses origin/lineage ('from the daughter of Mehrak'). The copula است appears sentence-final as in classical Persian to assert identity/state.
segments.s545-g66: ز (poetic preposition) marks source/agency 'from' or 'under the protection of'; پشت here means 'support/protection' and pairs with منست (an inflected/poetic form) and the following clause. این introduces 'this (one)' and is followed by م + را (split in the gloss) serving the object/topic role; بی‌شکست is a descriptive adjective 'undefeated/unbroken'. Overall the line says she is from my backing/support and that this one of mine is without defeat.
segments.s545-g67: ز ... و ز ... is a poetic repetition 'from the water and from the well.' آن 'that' is the demonstrative subject; کجا رفته بود uses the compound past (رفته بود) – a past perfect/pluperfect sense 'had gone' — forming a question about whereabouts.
segments.s545-g68: A simple sequence of actions joined by و: پسر (the young man) + گفت (said/spoke) + پرسید (asked). چندی 'for a while' modifies شنود; شنود is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person form meaning 'he listened' or 'hearing occurred.' The verbs describe sequential speech and listening.
segments.s545-g69: ز گفتار او 'from/because of his speech' gives the cause; شاد شد is a compound verb 'became glad' (شاد = happy, شد = became) and the subject اردشیر is placed after the verb for stylistic emphasis, a common classical word order.
segments.s545-g70: به + place (ایوان) marks motion toward/into 'the iwan'; خرامید is a past tense verb 'strode/pranced proudly' describing manner; خود is a reflexive/emphatic pronoun 'himself' reinforcing the subject; با وزیر 'with the vizier' is a standard prepositional accompaniment.
segments.s545-g71: گرفته is a perfect/participial form 'having taken' or 'seized'; دلاویز is the object 'the charming (one)' and is marked by را (definite object marker). بر کنار is a fixed adverbial phrase 'to one side / aside' describing the direction/result of the action.
segments.s545-g72: ز ایوان 'from the iwan' gives origin; سوی تخت 'toward the throne' gives destination; شد here functions as the verb indicating movement/occurred (classical usage where شد often marks the action), with شهریار (the king) placed finally as subject—typical epic word order.
segments.s545-g17: دوان شد is a two-part verbal phrase: دوان (run/rush) plus شد (past auxiliary) gives a sudden past action 'ran/rushed'. به میدان is a prepositional phrase 'into/to the arena/field'. شاه اردشیر is a title + name in apposition = 'King Ardashir' (title before the proper name).
segments.s545-g18: کمانی here names the object held — literally 'bow(-like)/of the bow', functioning as 'a bow'. به یک دست is a locative/instrumental 'in/with one hand' (elliptical 'in one hand'). و دیگر دو تیر continues the inventory: 'and (in) the other (hand) two arrows' — دیگر = 'the other'.
segments.s545-g19: ابا here functions as a proper name (not the lexical verb 'to refuse'); کودکان چند = 'several children' (چند as 'a few/several'). چوگان و گوی list game equipment: چوگان = 'polo mallet' and گوی = 'ball'. The conjunction و simply links the items.
segments.s545-g20: به میدان شاه اندر آمد is a motion clause: به میدان (into the field/arena) + شاه (the king's, here genitival/title) + اندر آمد (came in/entered; اندر is an emphatic/inward locative). ز کوی is the origin 'from the quarter/alley' (ز = 'from', poetic form).
segments.s545-g21: جهاندار = 'sovereign' (title). هم در زمان = 'also/at the same time' (هم = 'also/together', در زمان = 'at that time'). با سپاه = 'with an army/host' (با = 'with', سپاه = 'army'). The phrase sets actor, time, and accompaniment before the motion verb.
segments.s545-g22: به میدان بیامد is the motion verb complex 'came into the field' (به میدان = 'into the arena/field', بیامد = past 'came'). ز نخچیرگاه gives source 'from the hunting-ground' (ز = 'from', نخچیرگاه = hunting-ground).
segments.s545-g23: This nominal string lists figures: ابا (here a proper name — not the lexical 'to refuse'), موبدان (plural 'mobeds/priests'), موبد (the title 'mobed', here probably appositive 'the chief priest'), and تیزویر a proper name or epithet for that mobed. The sequence is a stacked apposition/naming of persons.
segments.s545-g24: به نزدیک ایوان رسید اردشیر is a motion/result clause: به نزدیک = 'to/near', ایوان = 'the iwan/portico', رسید = past 'reached/arrived', and اردشیر names the person who arrived (postverbal subject, a common poetic order).
segments.s545-g89: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' introducing a reported statement; گفته here is an impersonal/reporting participle 'it is said' (i.e. 'it is said that ...'); بد کید هندی is a noun phrase 'evil/ill plot of the Indian' that modifies the reported content; که introduces the subordinate clause whose subject is بخت.
segments.s545-g90: نگردد is a negative present/subjunctive 3rd-person form of گردیدن meaning 'may not/does not turn'; ترا is the enclitic object 'you' (ت + را, where ت = 2nd-person suffix and را = object marker); ساز و خرم به تخت is a predicative noun phrase ('saz and khorram' = music/cheer and joy) with به تخت marking the state/location 'on the throne' — together: 'fortune will not turn (to make) you joyful on the throne.'
segments.s545-g91: The repeated نه ... نه pattern lists items in negation ('neither ... nor ...'); کشور, افسر, گنج و سپاه are coordinated nouns (country/realm, helmet/crown, treasure and army) enumerated as things absent or lacking.
segments.s545-g92: Continuation of the negative list: دیهیم شاهی is a compound meaning 'rural/peasant kingship' (adjective before noun in classical order), and فر کلاه pairs the divine 'far' or radiance with 'khat'/'hat' (i.e. the crown's splendour); both items are negated by the leading نه.
segments.s545-g93: مگر is a concessive/exceptive particle here meaning 'unless' or 'except if'; تخمه مهرک is a genitive phrase 'the seed/offspring of Mehrak' and نوش‌زاد (postpositive adjective) means 'newborn' modifying تخمه.
segments.s545-g94: بیامیزد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive of آمیزدن 'to mingle/unite' (here subjunctive after مگر: 'should mingle'); آن دوده 'that soot' is the direct object, followed by با ان نژاد 'with that lineage' (با introducing the thing mixed with). Word order places demonstratives before the nouns.
segments.s545-g95: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame; سالیان 'years' with اندر آمد (lit. 'came in') is an idiomatic time expression meaning 'have come to (the number)'; به هشت marks the extent reached ('to eight' = eight years have elapsed).
segments.s545-g96: که introduces the subordinate clause describing those years: جز به آرزو 'except by/for wish' means 'apart from hopes', and چرخ بر ما نگشت is a negative past statement 'the wheel (of fate) did not turn for us' (نگشت = did not turn), with بر ما marking 'for us'.
segments.s545-g49: This clause places the verb-like form فروماند at the front, followed by the prepositional phrase زان کار ('from/at that deed') and the noun phrase گیتی شگفت ('the wondrous world'). In classical narrative Persian the verb (here passive/experiential) can precede its complements; the sense is that the world was overcome/astonished by that action. فروماند is an archaic/past-formal verbal adjective meaning 'was overcome' or 'was left/overcome (with feeling)'.
segments.s545-g50: بخندید is a simple past (or narrative past) intransitive verb 'he laughed'. The conjunction و links that action to the next predicate. اندیشه اندر گرفت is a light-verb construction where اندیشه ('thought') is the noun and اندر گرفت ('took in/ seized within') is the verbal part meaning 'thought took hold' or 'he became pensive' — a common classical way to say someone became absorbed in thought.
segments.s545-g51: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verbal form meaning 'he ordered/said' (classical usage). تا here works as a subordinating/sequence marker ('so that / then') introducing the following action; رفت is the past tense 'went', and شاپور پیش indicates the subject Shapur going 'before/in the presence (of someone)'. The verb and its complements follow customary classical word order.
segments.s545-g52: به پرسش گرفتش is an idiomatic sequence: به پرسش 'to/with a question' + گرفتش (lit. 'he seized him') here means 'he put him to the question' or 'questioned him'. ز + اندازه بیش ('concerning/ about the measure more') qualifies the question: 'about being more than the measure' (i.e. larger/excessive). The clause uses poetic, compact classical phrasing where ز governs the topic of the question.
segments.s545-g53: بترسید is the past-tense/perfect of ترسیدن 'to fear' and here is used with the subject شاپور — the sequence is Verb + Subject + Apposition (آزادمرد), a common poetic ordering that emphasizes the action. آزادمرد is an epithet/appositive meaning 'a noble/generous man' applied to Shapur.
segments.s545-g54: دلش گشت is a causal/resultative construction: دلش ('his heart') + گشت ('became') gives 'his heart became ...'. پردرد is an adjectival compound 'full of pain' meaning 'sorrowful', and the coordinating و links to رخساره زرد 'face/complexion (became) yellow/pale'. Gشت here is the simple past/become sense.
segments.s545-g55: بخندید is past tense 'he laughed'. The following short prepositional element ز (here contracted in the manuscript as زو) means 'at/from him' (i.e. 'he laughed at him'); نامور شهریار is an epithet-noun sequence 'the renowned/illustrious king' serving as the subject performing the laugh. Word order (verb then adverbial/agent) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s545-g56: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' where بدو = 'to him/unto him' and گفت is the past of گفتن 'to say' (here used in the simple narrative past). فرزند پنهان مدار is a direct imperative admonition: فرزند 'the child', پنهان 'hidden' (adjectival/participial), مدار functions here as a prohibitive/imperative admonition 'do not (make it) — do not hide'. This compact imperative phrase is typical of classical Persian: noun + participle + (negative) verb-form.
segments.s545-g25: بزد is a past action verb 'struck/ hit' placed before its actor and object (poetic word order). کودکی is an indefinite actor 'a boy/child'; نیز is the adverb 'also' modifying the whole clause. چوگان here names the activity/field (polo) rather than the mallet; ز + راه ('from the road/way') gives the direction/source of the stroke.
segments.s545-g26: بشد is a perfective/past-resultive verb 'it came to be / it happened' introducing the result. گوی گردان is a noun phrase with participial adjective: گوی 'the ball' + گردان 'turning/rolling' (describing the ball). به نزدیک شاه gives the goal/direction 'to near the king' (به + location/adverb + noun).
segments.s545-g27: نرفتند is the negative past 'they did not go / did not move'. زیشان is a fused prepositional phrase 'from them / away from it/them' specifying what they did not leave. پس is a discourse connector 'then/after that'; گوی کس is elliptical: گوی 'the ball' with کس 'anyone/no one' — the wording sets up that, after that, nobody (managed) the ball.
segments.s545-g28: بماندند is past 'they remained / were left' (third person plural). بر جای is a fixed locative 'on the spot / in their places'. ناکام is an adjective 'unsuccessful / thwarted' describing their state; بس here is a closing particle meaning roughly 'enough' or an interjectional 'so it was' emphasising the finality of their failure.
segments.s545-g29: دوان is a verbal form meaning 'ran / rushed' (present/past participial flavor used as a simple past/action). اورمزد is the subject (Ahura Mazda). از میانه is a prepositional source 'from the midst/center'. برفت is an archaic past 'went forth' (classical compound of بر + رفت) marking the completion of the move.
segments.s545-g30: به پیش جهاندار is a prepositional phrase 'to the presence of the sovereign' (به + پیش = 'to before/in the presence of' + جهاندار 'the ruler'). چون introduces a simile 'like / as'; باد is the image 'wind', and تفت is an intensifying/imagistic word here (the poetical noun/verb creating the sense of a swift, blasting motion).
segments.s545-g31: ز پیش is a locative 'from before / out of the front (i.e. from the king's presence)'. نیا here functions as a personal name or character identifier (treated syntactically as the subject who acts). زود برداشت is the verbal complex 'quickly took up / picked up' (زود = adverb 'quickly', برداشت = past 'lifted/removed'). گوی is the direct object 'the ball'.
segments.s545-g32: ازو گشت is 'from him / because of him it became' (ازو = 'from him/of him', گشت = 'became / turned'). لشکر پر از گفت‌وگوی is a noun phrase 'the host/army full of talk' (لشکر 'army', پر 'full (of)', از + گفت‌وگوی = 'of conversation/discussion'), indicating that his action set the army talking.
segments.s545-g1: بسی is an adverbial modifier placed before the verb and here qualifies frequency/intensity; برنیامد is a past tense, impersonal/compound verbal phrase meaning 'did not come about / did not happen much' (the negative sense is lexicalized in the gerundial form here), and برین روزگار is a locative/time phrase ('in these days') where بر = 'on/upon' + این (here written ین) + روزگار = 'time/days'. The whole segment functions as an impersonal clause 'it did not happen much in these days.'
segments.s545-g2: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/which'; سرو سهی is a noun phrase ('a cypress' + a poetic epithet) with the epithet following the noun as in classical Persian; چون گل is a simile 'like a flower'; آمد به بار is a fixed verbal phrase 'to come to fruit / to become gravid / to bear' with آمد as past-tense verb and به marking the relation to بار (the result/occasion).
segments.s545-g3: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; نه ماه is a numeral + noun ('nine months') with the numeral preceding the noun; بگذشت is the simple past 'passed' (of time); بر + ماه‌روی is a locative/relational phrase literally 'over the moon-faced (woman)' (بر = 'upon/over', ماه‌رو = 'moon-faced, beautiful‑faced').
segments.s545-g4: یکی is an indefinite numeral here 'one/a certain'; کودک is the subject 'child/infant'; آمد is the past-tense verb 'came/appeared' (used for the child's arrival/birth); به بالای اوی is a prepositional phrase 'to her bosom/onto her lap' where به = 'to', بالای = 'the upper place/above' and اوی is the enclitic referring back to the woman ('his/her').
segments.s545-g5: تو is the second‑person singular pronoun 'you'; گفتی here is the past tense verb 'you said' (the lemma list given for گفتی is nounish, so the verbal sense is not among the provided senses); که introduces the reported content; بازآمد is the past tense 'returned/come back' and اسفندیار is the proper name, the subject of بازآمد.
segments.s545-g6: وگر is a compact classical particle (built from و + گر) used here to add/turn to the next item 'and (also)'; نامدار is an adjective 'renowned/illustrious' modifying اردشیر; اردشیر is the name; سوار is a noun/adjective 'rider, mounted one/horseman' completing the description. The sequence is a terse nominative phrase appended to the report.
segments.s545-g7: ورا is an enclitic prepositional phrase 'to him' (ورا = به + او); نام شاپور کرد is a causative/relational sequence where نام = 'name' (object), شاپور is the name given, and کرد is the past-tense verb 'made/gave' — together 'made his name Shāpūr'; اورمزد (Ahura Mazda) is the agent and appears postverbally for emphasis (typical in classical Persian word order).
segments.s545-g8: که introduces an explanatory/result clause 'that/for/so that'; سروی is a simile/metaphor noun 'a cypress' (used figuratively for a tall/graceful youth); بد here is an adjectival element whose basic listed senses are 'bad/ill' but in classical poetic usage it can be emphatic or have non‑literal valences (the exact sense is ambiguous in the list); اندر میان is a fixed locative series 'in/within the midst of' (اندر = 'in/within', میان = 'middle/amidst'); فرزد is 'son/youth' — together 'that he was a cypress among the youths.'
segments.s545-g81: به دیبا (a prepositional/instrumental phrase) marks the material or manner: 'with/in brocade'. بیاراست is a past tense (3rd‑person) verb 'adorned/made beautiful'. آتشکده is the direct object placed after the verb here by poetic word order; literally: 'with brocade adorned (he) the fire‑temple.'
segments.s545-g82: هم begins the clause as 'also/likewise' linking this noun phrase to the previous action. ایوان نوروز is a noun‑noun genitive/attributive compound 'the Iwan (portico) of Nowruz' and و coordinates that with کاخ سده 'the palace of Sadeh'; the whole phrase means 'and also the Iwan of Nowruz and the Sadeh palace.'
segments.s545-g83: یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone' (used here as 'one/so‑and‑so'); بزمگه is the object 'banquet‑hall'; ساخت is the past 3rd‑person 'built'. با + مهتران is an accompanying prepositional phrase 'with the mahterān/with the chiefs' placed after the verb for emphasis — in context it links to the following verb where those people appear as participants.
segments.s545-g84: نشستند is the past 3rd‑plural 'they sat' (verb applies to the people introduced before); هرجای is a locative adverb 'wherever/everywhere'; رامشگران is the plural noun 'peacemakers/comforters'. The verb precedes the locative phrase here because of poetic inversion: 'they sat wherever the peacemakers were' / 'the peacemakers sat everywhere.'
segments.s545-g85: چنین گفت is a fixed (poetic) phrase 'thus he said' or 'he said this' — گفت here is the past verb 'said'. با نامداران شهر is the indirect object 'to/with the city's notables' (نامداران = the famed/important people of the city). Grammatically this is a reported statement introduced to the city notables.
segments.s545-g86: هرانکس که introduces a relative clause 'whoever / anyone who', with که as the relative 'who/that'. او از خرد داشت is a semi‑idiomatic construction 'he had of wisdom' = 'he had wisdom' (داشت is past 'had'); بهر is an adverb/preposition 'for / toward / as to' that here leads into the purpose or beneficiary picked up by the following clause. The whole unit functions as 'whoever had any wisdom (for)...'.
segments.s545-g87: که is a subordinating conjunction ('that' / 'for') linking this clause to what precedes. از گفت is a genitive/source phrase 'from the saying' = 'according to what was said'. دانا modifies the following noun phrase as an adjective 'wise'. ستاره شمر functions as a compound noun phrase 'star-reader' / 'star-counting one' (an astrologer/astronomer) in archaic/poetic wording; word order keeps the adjective before the noun phrase it qualifies.
segments.s545-g88: نباید is an impersonal modal negative 'it must not / one must not' introducing prohibition. که again introduces the subordinate clause. هرگز is an adverb 'never'. کند is the present/subjunctive/optative form of کردن 'to do/make' used here with the noun گذر to mean 'let/make passage' (classical word order places this verb before its subject). کس is the indefinite 'anyone/someone'. گذر is a noun 'passage' used as the action that would be 'made' — together کند کس گذر = 'that anyone should pass / that anyone should be allowed passage.'
segments.s545-g73: بیاراست is a past, 3rd-person singular verbal form (from بیاراستن) placed before its objects — a common poetic word order (verb-first). زرین یکی زیرگاه is a noun phrase where یکی functions as an indefinite numeral/adjective “a certain / one” attached to زیرگاه (“footstool / pedestal”); زرین (“golden”) is an adjective modifying that noun. The whole segment therefore reads “he adorned/set in order a golden footstool.”
segments.s545-g74: یکی طوق فرمود places the indefinite یکی before طوق (“a collar/necklet”), then the verb فرمود (past 3rd-sing) meaning “he ordered / commanded (to be made)”; in classical Persian فرمود often means ‘he bade/caused’. The coordinating و links that clause to زرین کلاه, where زرین precedes کلاه by poetic inversion (adjective before noun) to mean “a golden cap/hat.”
segments.s545-g75: سر خرد کودک is a genitive/attributive chain meaning “the small head of the child,” where خرد here is the older/poetic sense “small” (not ‘wisdom’). The verb بیاراستند is past, 3rd-person plural (“they adorned”), placed after its object as is normal in Persian.
segments.s545-g76: بس is an adverbial quantifier here meaning ‘much / plentiful’ (archaic/intensifying use). از is the preposition “from,” and the phrase گنج در is a poetic compound referring to the treasury/treasure-house; در participates in that compound. و connects to گهر (“gems, jewels”), and خواستند is the past, 3rd-person plural verb “they requested/they asked for.”
segments.s545-g77: همی ریخت combines همی (an iterative/continuous adverb meaning ‘kept on / continuously’) with ریخت (past 3rd-singular, here ‘poured / fell’ — used intransitively to indicate something kept falling/streaming). تا introduces a temporal/result clause “until,” شد is the auxiliary/verb “became,” سرش is ‘his head’ (سر + enclitic ش = his), and ناپدید is the adjective ‘vanished/invisible.’
segments.s545-g78: تنش را is the direct object ('his body' + object marker را). نیا here functions as a noun/title (an elder or named person, Niyâ) who acts as the subject. زان میان is a prepositional expression ‘from that midst / out of among them’ (زان = 'from that'), and برکشید is the past 3rd-singular verb ‘drew up / lifted out.’ The clause thus means ‘Niyâ drew his body out from among them.’
segments.s545-g79: بسی is an intensifying quantifier meaning ‘much / a great deal (of).’ زر و گوهر is a coordinated noun phrase ‘gold and gems.’ به دَرویش is the indirect object introduced by the preposition به (‘to/for the dervish’). داد is the past 3rd-person singular verb ‘gave.’
segments.s545-g80: خردمند را is a topicalized object phrase (‘the wise man’ + object marker را). خواسته here is a past-participial/adjectival use meaning ‘(what) was asked’ or ‘he had wished for’; بیش is an adverb ‘more/extra’; and the final داد is the main verb ‘gave.’ The literal order is ‘to the wise man, what was asked, more, he gave,’ i.e. he gave the wise man more than he had asked.
segments.s545-g41: بشد is the past form of شدن used here as 'he rose/it happened' (classical past); موبد is the subject 'the mobad/priest'. The sequence و + برگرفتش links the next action: برگرفتش is برگرفت + ـش (enclitic 'him'), 'he took/raised him', and ز گرد means 'from the throng/from around' (ز = 'from', گرد here = the crowd/around).
segments.s545-g42: ببردش is a verb with the enclitic pronoun ـش 'him' attached, meaning 'he carried/took him' (classical narrative past). بر شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward the king' (بر = toward), and آزادمرد is an epithet modifying شاه 'the noble/generous man (king)'.
segments.s545-g43: بدو is the dative 'to him/unto him' (to indicate the indirect object). The sequence بدو گفت شاه is a poetic ordering meaning 'the king said to him'. این گرانمایه خرد is the noun phrase 'this precious wisdom' (این = 'this', گرانمایه = 'precious/esteemed', خرد = 'wisdom').
segments.s545-g44: ترا here is split orthographically (ت + را): ت is the 2nd-person suffix element and را is the object marker, giving 'you' as the affected person. از نژاد = 'from/of (a) lineage' (از = 'from', نژاد = 'lineage/race'), و که باید شمرد is a relative clause 'that must be (or should be) counted/reckoned' (باید = 'must/ought', شمرد = 'to count/reckon'). The clause as a whole attributes noble lineage to the addressee.
segments.s545-g45: نترسید is the negative/past sense 'did not fear' (used adverbially here: 'unafraid'). کودک is the subject 'the child'. به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'in a voice/aloud' (به + آواز), and گفت is the verb 'said' (poetic V–S order: verb before subject).
segments.s545-g46: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. نام نژادم = 'the name of my lineage' (نام = 'name', نژادم = نژاد + ـم 'my lineage'). نباید here is impersonal 'should not / must not', and نهفت is used in the sense 'be hidden/concealed' (passive sense 'be hidden'). Together: 'that the name of my lineage should not be hidden.'
segments.s545-g47: منم is the fused form of من هستم 'I am' (here assertive 'I am'). پور شاپور = 'the son of Shapur' (پور = 'son/descendant', شاپور = proper name). کو is an archaic/poetic relative/interrogative particle ('who/which') linking to the following phrase; پور تست here is an older/clipped expression in which تست functions to relate 'is/are' to the second person (classical contraction), so the cluster identifies Shapur as related to the addressee. The line uses classical, compact genealogy formulas common in epic Persian.
segments.s545-g48: ز فرزند = 'from the son/descendant of' (ز = 'from/of', فرزند = 'son/descendant'). مهرک is a personal name (Mehrak). نژاد درست = 'of true/proper lineage' (نژاد = 'lineage', درست = 'correct/proper'), an emphatic closing that asserts legitimate descent.

sentences.s545-s17: After that a loud cry was raised; from it the fortunate, victorious king became astonished.
sentences.s545-s18: He said to the mōbed: 'Keep watch on this noble-born man and see from whom he has his lineage.'
sentences.s545-s19: The mōbed asked, but no one knew; everyone chose silence, and that was enough.
sentences.s545-s20: Then the king said to the mōbed: 'Bring his brother up from the ground and bring him before me.'
sentences.s545-s5: Thus, until those seven years had come and gone, Ahura Mazda remained in the world without respite.
sentences.s545-s6: They kept him hidden from everyone and did not leave him anywhere for play.
sentences.s545-s7: For seven days Ardashir became the quarry; likewise Shapur became the hunter.
sentences.s545-s8: Secretly Ahura Mazda came forth from among the group, for he had grown weary of teaching.
sentences.s545-s29: It is fitting that the son should be of anyone; they say that this child is a king.
sentences.s545-s30: Shapur said to him, 'You gave a draught; you gave the world provisions for the meeting.'
sentences.s545-s31: This is from my back (from me) and the name is Ohrmazd; radiant, like a tulip among youths.
sentences.s545-s32: I kept my disgust hidden from the king until it came to light concerning the fruit-bearer.
sentences.s545-s49: When Shapur went into his own repose, he saw nothing of the world except his own desire.
sentences.s545-s50: The seven countries of the earth became mine indeed; my heart obtained from fate something that it desired.
sentences.s545-s51: And thereafter, his officials (or ministers) were given the title; they made him Shahanshah.
sentences.s545-s33: She is a precious one, the daughter of Mehrak; she is under my protection — this of mine is undefeated.
sentences.s545-s34: From the water and from the well — where had that one gone? The youth spoke and asked, and listened for a while.
sentences.s545-s35: Ardashir rejoiced at his words, and he proudly strode into the iwan with the vizier.
sentences.s545-s36: Having taken the charming one aside, the king moved from the iwan toward the throne.
sentences.s545-s9: King Ardashir ran swiftly into the arena, a bow in one hand and two arrows in the other.
sentences.s545-s10: Aba, with a few children and a mallet and the ball, came into the king's field from the quarter.
sentences.s545-s11: The sovereign, too, at that time with his host came into the field from the hunting-ground.
sentences.s545-s12: Aba, the mobeds and the chief mobed Tizwīr; Ardashir reached near the iwan.
sentences.s545-s45: Thus it is said — the evil Indian plot says — that fortune will not turn to make you joyous and prosperous on the throne.
sentences.s545-s46: Neither realm nor helmet, neither treasure nor army; neither rustic kingship nor the splendour of the crown.
sentences.s545-s47: Except if the offspring of Mehrak, newly born, should mingle that soot with that lineage.
sentences.s545-s48: Now eight years have come (i.e. it has been eight years) during which, except for longing/wishes, the wheel of fate has not turned for us.
sentences.s545-s25: The world was astonished at that deed; he laughed and became thoughtful.
sentences.s545-s26: He gave the order, and Shapur went forward; he put him to the question about being beyond the measure.
sentences.s545-s27: Shapur, the noble man, grew afraid; his heart became sorrowful and his face grew pale.
sentences.s545-s28: The famed king laughed at him and said to him, 'Do not hide the child.'
sentences.s545-s13: A boy likewise struck in the polo game from the road; the rolling ball came close to the king.
sentences.s545-s14: They did not move away from it; afterwards no one (took the) ball — they remained on their places, disappointed.
sentences.s545-s15: Ahura Mazda ran forth from the middle and went before the sovereign like a gust of wind.
sentences.s545-s16: From before (the king) Niyâ quickly picked up the ball; from him the host became full of talk.
sentences.s545-s1: In those days it was not often that a dark, graceful cypress came to bear like a flower.
sentences.s545-s2: When nine months had passed over the moon‑faced one, a child came to her bosom.
sentences.s545-s3: You said that Esfandiyār returned, and that Ardashir was a famed horseman.
sentences.s545-s4: Ahura Mazda gave him the name Shāpūr, for he stood like a cypress among the youths.
sentences.s545-s41: He adorned the fire‑temple with brocade, and likewise the Iwan of Nowruz and the palace of Sadeh.
sentences.s545-s42: Someone built a banquet‑hall; the peacemakers sat everywhere together with the chiefs.
sentences.s545-s43: He said this to the city's notables: whoever had any wisdom...
sentences.s545-s44: For, as the wise star-reader said, no one should ever be allowed to pass.
sentences.s545-s37: He set a golden footstool in place, ordered a collar, and a golden cap.
sentences.s545-s38: They adorned the child's small head; they asked for much from the treasury and for gems.
sentences.s545-s39: It kept pouring until his head vanished; the elder Niyâ drew his body out from among them.
sentences.s545-s40: He gave the dervish much gold and jewels; to the wise man he gave more than he had asked.
sentences.s545-s21: The mobad rose, lifted him out of the throng, and carried him to the noble king.
sentences.s545-s22: The king said to him, 'This precious wisdom shows you are of a lineage that should be reckoned.'
sentences.s545-s23: The child, not afraid, spoke aloud: 'The name of my lineage should not be hidden.'
sentences.s545-s24: I am the son of Shapur (who is your son), descended from Mehrak's son of true lineage.
